scenarioName: Aortic Dissection — Tearing Chest/Back Pain with BP Differential, Pain Control, Gentle Transport

scenarioStory.history: 65-year-old male was loading split firewood from the bed of his pickup truck onto a hand cart when, mid-lift, he was struck with a sudden, severe tearing pain in his anterior chest that immediately radiated through to his upper back between the shoulder blades. He describes it as 'like something ripped inside me' and rates it 10/10. He fell to one knee against the truck and could not move further. He felt diaphoretic and lightheaded but did not lose consciousness. His wife was on the porch and ran out, then called 911. He has had hypertension for at least 20 years that he 'mostly takes medicine for' but admits he runs out occasionally and has not had a clinic visit in 14 months. Long-time smoker (40+ pack-years, currently 1 ppd). His father died at age 62 of 'something with his heart, they couldn't fix it' — no other family details. Denies recent illness, denies prior similar pain, denies trauma other than the lifting effort. No use of cocaine or stimulants.
scenarioStory.course: Initial Contact: EMS finds a 65-year-old man with classic features of acute aortic dissection: SUDDEN tearing pain radiating from anterior chest to interscapular back, severe HTN with longstanding poor control, heavy smoking history, and family history of premature cardiac death. Critical EMS exam findings: a measurable BP DIFFERENTIAL between arms (right arm 198/110, left arm 152/90 — a >40 mmHg gap), and a subtle left-arm grip weakness with intact right grip. 12-lead shows sinus rhythm at 92 with NO ST elevation and only nonspecific changes — this is NOT STEMI. The protocol pathway here is recognition of the pattern, OPIOID pain control (fentanyl preferred), CAUTIOUS BP reduction toward target SBP 100–120 to lower wall shear stress, GENTLE transport with minimal jostling, and routing to a CARDIOTHORACIC/VASCULAR-SURGICAL center — not the closest hospital. Aspirin is deferred or held pending physician guidance because this is not ACS and antiplatelet therapy can worsen bleeding if the dissection ruptures into the pericardium. → Fentanyl given; gentle transport prep: Crew gives fentanyl 95 mcg IV (calc: 95 kg × 1 mcg/kg = 95 mcg, capped at 100 mcg max). Pain reduces from 10/10 to 6/10 within 5 minutes and BP softens with sympathetic tone reduction (right arm 168/96, left arm 138/82 — differential narrows but persists). Aspirin held; medical control consulted and concurs with deferring antiplatelet therapy and routing to the regional cardiothoracic surgery center despite the longer transport (~25 minutes) versus the closer community ED (~10 minutes). Crew lifts and moves the patient with minimal jostling, maintaining a head-up position of comfort, and transports gently — every bump and accel/decel adds shear stress to a tearing aorta. → Stable on analgesia; gentle transport to vascular center: Patient transported gently to regional cardiothoracic/vascular surgical center. Driver coached to drive smoothly — no hard accel/decel, no rough lane changes, cushion every bump. Pain reassessed every 5 minutes; fentanyl repeated once at half-dose for breakthrough pain (50 mcg given) per protocol allowance with VS adequate. Continuous monitoring includes bilateral BPs every 5 minutes, pulse symmetry checks, neurologic status, and serial 12-leads. Receiving facility has prepped CT angiography on the gantry and the cardiothoracic surgery team is mobilized.

patientInformation.patientCondition: Sudden, severe tearing chest pain that started about 35 minutes ago while loading firewood, radiating from his anterior chest through to between his shoulder blades; rated 10/10 and described as 'ripping.'

scenarioEvents.name: Initial Contact
scenarioEvents.description: EMS finds a 65-year-old man with classic features of acute aortic dissection: SUDDEN tearing pain radiating from anterior chest to interscapular back, severe HTN with longstanding poor control, heavy smoking history, and family history of premature cardiac death. Critical EMS exam findings: a measurable BP DIFFERENTIAL between arms (right arm 198/110, left arm 152/90 — a >40 mmHg gap), and a subtle left-arm grip weakness with intact right grip. 12-lead shows sinus rhythm at 92 with NO ST elevation and only nonspecific changes — this is NOT STEMI. The protocol pathway here is recognition of the pattern, OPIOID pain control (fentanyl preferred), CAUTIOUS BP reduction toward target SBP 100–120 to lower wall shear stress, GENTLE transport with minimal jostling, and routing to a CARDIOTHORACIC/VASCULAR-SURGICAL center — not the closest hospital. Aspirin is deferred or held pending physician guidance because this is not ACS and antiplatelet therapy can worsen bleeding if the dissection ruptures into the pericardium.
relatedChecklist: Recognize tearing-pain + back radiation + risk factors → high suspicion aortic dissection
BP BOTH arms; check pulse symmetry; document a >20 mmHg differential as key finding
12-lead EKG; look for STEMI; here: NO ST elevation — do NOT activate STEMI alert
IV access ×2; cardiac monitor; SpO2; defib pads on (high-risk patient)
Fentanyl 95 mcg IV (95 kg × 1 mcg/kg = 95 mcg, max 100 mcg) — analgesia FIRST
Defer/hold aspirin pending dissection workup; consult medical control or receiving MD

scenarioEvents.name: Fentanyl given; gentle transport prep
scenarioEvents.description: Crew gives fentanyl 95 mcg IV (calc: 95 kg × 1 mcg/kg = 95 mcg, capped at 100 mcg max). Pain reduces from 10/10 to 6/10 within 5 minutes and BP softens with sympathetic tone reduction (right arm 168/96, left arm 138/82 — differential narrows but persists). Aspirin held; medical control consulted and concurs with deferring antiplatelet therapy and routing to the regional cardiothoracic surgery center despite the longer transport (~25 minutes) versus the closer community ED (~10 minutes). Crew lifts and moves the patient with minimal jostling, maintaining a head-up position of comfort, and transports gently — every bump and accel/decel adds shear stress to a tearing aorta.
relatedChecklist: Reassess pain (10/10 → 6/10); repeat fentanyl q5 min PRN if VS allow (max as protocol)
Consider NTG 0.4 mg SL ONCE for SBP control (target 100–120) — only if pain inadequately controlled and SBP >100
Consider metoprolol 5 mg IV slow (SBP >120) for HR/dP-dt reduction — if available and per medical control
HOB 30°; padded stretcher; minimize jostling — wall shear is the killer
Transport to cardiothoracic/vascular surgery center — bypass closest if necessary
Advance notice: 'Suspected aortic dissection — BP differential, tearing pain, no STEMI'

scenarioEvents.name: Stable on analgesia; gentle transport to vascular center
scenarioEvents.description: Patient transported gently to regional cardiothoracic/vascular surgical center. Driver coached to drive smoothly — no hard accel/decel, no rough lane changes, cushion every bump. Pain reassessed every 5 minutes; fentanyl repeated once at half-dose for breakthrough pain (50 mcg given) per protocol allowance with VS adequate. Continuous monitoring includes bilateral BPs every 5 minutes, pulse symmetry checks, neurologic status, and serial 12-leads. Receiving facility has prepped CT angiography on the gantry and the cardiothoracic surgery team is mobilized.
relatedChecklist: Bilateral BPs q5 min; document pulse symmetry; flag widening differential
Serial 12-leads — watch for new ST changes (proximal extension into coronaries)
Serial neuro checks: grip, facial droop, speech, mentation — extension marker
Bedside handoff with bilateral BP trend, fentanyl timeline, neuro exam, EKG strips

scenarioEvents.name: Treated as STEMI/ACS; bilateral BP missed
scenarioEvents.description: Crew anchored on 'chest pain = MI' without performing bilateral BPs and pattern-matched to ACS: aspirin 324 mg PO chewed, NTG 0.4 mg SL, and routing to the closest community ED (no cardiothoracic surgical capability). Within 10 minutes en route, the dissection extends — pain abruptly recurs, the right arm BP shoots up while the left becomes weak, the patient develops a new diastolic murmur (proximal extension causing aortic insufficiency), and mentation begins to cloud as cerebral perfusion suffers. The community ED, on receiving the patient, has to recognize the dissection on CT and arrange a critical-care interfacility transfer to a surgical center — a 60–90 minute delay in definitive therapy. The teaching points are: (1) bilateral BPs are the EMS-level discriminator that prevents this anchoring error; (2) destination decisions matter; (3) aspirin and antiplatelets in an undifferentiated severe-pain patient with risk factors for dissection can worsen bleeding if rupture occurs.
relatedChecklist: Recognize anchoring error: bilateral BPs now confirm dissection — redirect care plan
Fentanyl 95 mcg IV NOW (95 kg × 1 mcg/kg; max 100 mcg) for renewed 9/10 pain
Notify receiving ED of dissection findings; request immediate transfer arrangement on arrival
Medical control consult: BP control with metoprolol IV (SBP >120) for dP/dt reduction
Document timeline; debrief: bilateral BPs and tearing-pain pattern as dissection-vs-MI rule
